citations: Keane (2011) on labor supply elasticities

citations: Eissa & Liebman (1996) - Tax Refund and Labor Supply of Low-Income Mothers
Saez, Slemrod & Giertz (2012) - Elasticity of Taxable Income
Blundell & MaCurdy (1999) - Labor Supply: A Review of Alternative Approaches
runs.reasoningSummary: Decile 6 represents middle-income primary earners (~50th percentile). Literature on taxable income elasticity and labor supply suggests substitution elasticities for this group are modest. Primary earners (typically higher attachment) have lower elasticities than secondary earners. Decile 6 is above the very low-income range where EITC effects are largest, but below high-income thresholds where elasticities tend to be lower. I estimate a central elasticity around 0.25, with meaningful uncertainty reflecting heterogeneity in responses and tax-benefit design variations.
